serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tom Kha Gai is approximately 350 g, typically served as a bowl of soup with chicken pieces in a coconut milk broth.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Kha Gai (Thai coconut milk chicken soup) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai coconut-milk-based soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Tom Kha Gai as a composite dish. Values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition: chicken (~30%), coconut milk (~35%), broth/water (~25%), vegetables and aromatics (~10%), using USDA SR Legacy entries for chicken (cooked, skinless), coconut milk (canned), mushrooms, and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Thai soups. Energy and macros are consistent with published Thai food composition references for coconut-milk chicken soups (~85–100 kcal/100g).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coconut milk-based soups and mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on coconut milk curry and chicken soup GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Tom Kha Gai exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous coconut milk-based Thai curry soups and mixed chicken dishes with low carbohydrate content (~2.8g/100g). Coconut milk has a low GI (~40) and the dish is predominantly fat and protein with minimal starchy carbohydrates. GL per serving = (38 × 9.8g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 3.7, confirming very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g; ~1120 mg per 350g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Tom Kha Gai contains approximately 1120 mg sodium per standard serving primarily from fish sauce and broth, which represents nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume a smaller portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (~2.8g/100g) and low glycemic index (~38)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom Kha Gai has a low glycemic index of approximately 38 and a very low glycemic load of 3.7 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.5g/100g) warrants attention for overall dietary fat management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.5g/100g saturated fat)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk base contributes significant saturated fat (~19.3g per 350g serving), which exceeds the recommended daily saturated fat intake for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or choose a reduced-coconut-milk preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Kha Gai (ต้มข่าไก่) is a classic Thai coconut milk soup featuring tender chicken pieces simmered in a fragrant broth made from coconut milk, galangal (kha), lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, mushrooms, fish sauce, lime juice, and fresh chilli. The name translates literally as 'boiled galangal chicken.' The soup has a rich, creamy texture from the coconut milk, with a complex flavour profile that balances sour (lime), salty (fish sauce), spicy (chilli), and aromatic (galangal, lemongrass) notes. It is a staple of Thai cuisine and widely served in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, Tom Kha Gai is moderate in energy (~92 kcal/100g), high in fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk at ~5.5g/100g), moderate in protein (~6.5g/100g), and very low in carbohydrates (~2.8g/100g). Its low glycemic index (~38) and very low glycemic load (GL ~3.7 per serving) make it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is its sodium content (~1120 mg per serving from fish sauce) and saturated fat load (~19g per serving), which are relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management respectively. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for Tom Kha Gai as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable coconut milk-based dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-established and the estimation methodology is defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in coconut milk and chicken-based preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. The food_name in the input record ('212D Tom Kha Chicken Soup with Coconut M') appears to be a menu item code with truncated name; canonical name has been normalised to 'Tom Kha Gai (Thai Coconut Milk Chicken Soup)'. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous coconut milk-based Thai dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Tom Kha Gai was identified. Sodium estimate (~320 mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with fish sauce; home preparations may vary. Halal status assumes halal-certified chicken and fish sauce as served at Sanook Kitchen - Funan (field survey data source). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are null as Tom Kha Gai is a Thai dish and these language fields are mandatory only for Singaporean dishes; the Thai name (ต้มข่าไก่) satisfies the G6 multilingual requirement for Thai cuisine.
